**Juan Cruz Real** (born 8 October 1976) is an Argentine [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") manager and former player who played as a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder").

Real had numerous stints in South American leagues, and finished off his career in the [Canadian Professional Soccer League](/wiki/Canadian_Professional_Soccer_League_(1998-2005) "Canadian Professional Soccer League (1998-2005)").

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juan_Cruz_Real&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

Real began his career in his native country with Argentinian giants [Independiente](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Independiente "Club Atlético Independiente").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1997, he signed with [Deportivo Español](/wiki/Deportivo_Espa%C3%B1ol "Deportivo Español"), and the following season he went to Colombia to play for [Millonarios F.C.](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.") In 1999, he returned to Argentina to sign with [Arsenal de Sarandí](/wiki/Arsenal_de_Sarand%C3%AD "Arsenal de Sarandí"). Soon after he featured in the lower Argentinian leagues with [Independiente Rivadavia](/wiki/Independiente_Rivadavia "Independiente Rivadavia"), and [Almirante Brown de Arrecifes](/wiki/Almirante_Brown_de_Arrecifes "Almirante Brown de Arrecifes"), winning the [Torneo Argentino A](/wiki/Torneo_Argentino_A "Torneo Argentino A") in 1999. Cruz Real had a stint overseas in the island of Haiti with [Roulado](/wiki/Roulado_FC "Roulado FC") in 2002, and returning to the South American continent in 2003 with [Unión San Felipe](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_San_Felipe "Unión San Felipe") of Chile.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2004, he went once more overseas to Canada to sign with the [Brampton Hitmen](/wiki/Brampton_Hitmen "Brampton Hitmen") of the [Canadian Professional Soccer League](/wiki/Canadian_Professional_Soccer_League_(1998-2005) "Canadian Professional Soccer League (1998-2005)"). He made his debut for the club on September 10, 2004, in a match against [Toronto Supra](/wiki/SC_Toronto "SC Toronto").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He helped Brampton secure a post-season berth by finishing fourth in the Western Conference. He featured in the wildcard playoff match against [Toronto Croatia](/wiki/Toronto_Croatia "Toronto Croatia"), but were eliminated by a score of 3–1.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The following year he signed with division rivals [Hamilton Thunder](/wiki/Hamilton_Thunder "Hamilton Thunder").[[6]](#cite_note-6) His achievements with Hamilton were clinching the division title, and qualifying for the post-season. He featured in the semifinal playoff match against [Oakville Blue Devils](/wiki/Brampton_United "Brampton United"), but lost by a score of 2–0.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2009, he had a stint with [North York Astros](/wiki/North_York_Astros "North York Astros") in the [Canadian Soccer League](/wiki/Canadian_Soccer_League "Canadian Soccer League").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juan_Cruz_Real&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

Cruz Real was named the manager for [Deportes Tolima](/wiki/Deportes_Tolima "Deportes Tolima") in 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 2024, he returned to Argentina to be named the manager of [Club Atlético Belgrano](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Belgrano "Club Atlético Belgrano").[[12]](#cite_note-12) After nine months with Belgrano, it was mutually decided to relieve him.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In December 2025, Real was appointed as manager of [Universidad de Concepción](/wiki/C.D._Universidad_de_Concepci%C3%B3n "C.D. Universidad de Concepción") in the [Chilean Primera División](/wiki/Liga_de_Primera "Liga de Primera").[[14]](#cite_note-14) He left them in March 2026.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
